A razor sharp, torturous experience, keeping you guessing to the very end. All involved deliver their best. What feels like Fincher's true debut (after all the tampering of Aliens 3) immediately solidifies him as a visionary and auteur in the cruelty of the human psyche. As for the movie, it's one of the most brilliantly twisted crime stories ever made, with a mammoth twist that will leave you shaken. Psychological thrillers don't come any better than this.

Se7en is a masterclass in darkness and tension. From the moment you step into Fincher’s rain-soaked, claustrophobic city, you know you’re in for a ride. Brad Pitt and Morgan Freeman have brilliant chemistry — Pitt’s impulsive energy bouncing off Freeman’s calm, methodical intellect. Kevin Spacey is quietly terrifying, even before the finale. The investigation is slow, meticulous, and full of dread, and every clue and encounter ratchets up the tension. And then the ending — brutal, shocking, unforgettable. It punches your brain in the gut, leaves you thinking about it for days, and perfectly encapsulates the film’s grim worldview. Stylish, clever, and relentless, Se7en is one of those movies that feels like a storm you can’t escape, and you wouldn’t want to.
If I could give it seven stars I would;) great movie amazing acting and just perfect story start to finish.
this move is absolutely astounding. honestly such a great watch, if you’re looking for a great way to spend the night and then staying up late because you can’t sleep this is the movie for you.

Se7en (1995) is a relentlessly grim, immaculately crafted descent into moral rot that still feels disturbingly modern. Donald Trump’s United States. Powered by the quiet gravitas of Morgan Freeman and the raw volatility of Brad Pitt, the film builds dread through methodical detective work rather than cheap shocks, culminating in an ending that refuses any comforting resolution. David Fincher’s meticulous direction and Darius Khondji’s filthy, rain‑soaked visuals turn the unnamed city into a character of pure decay, while the seven deadly sins framework gives the story an almost biblical weight. If you want to dive deeper into how the film’s cinematography, sound design, and performances all work together to make Se7en a true genre landmark, check out my full breakdown on YouTube (certifiedfreshmovies) for a more detailed analysis and behind‑the‑scenes context.

Se7en is an exceptionally well-written, groundbreaking thriller that delves into the darkest aspects of human nature, presenting a complex and suspenseful murder mystery that showcases the talents of its esteemed cast, while also solidifying David Fincher's reputation as a pioneering filmmaker, especially during the 90s, a decade that saw the film reach its peak. Grading: A+

A fascinatingly gruesome murder mystery crime thriller that you can't stop watching. I was on the edge of my seat the entire time waiting to see what happens next. It's excellently directed with perfect pacing, cinematography, and set design. Morgan Freeman plays against type as the cynical and tired old detective training a VERY young Brad Pitt as the rookie. R Lee Ermey also plays against type as the more subdued and gentle police captain. It's a creatively brutal premise with the John Doe killer's crimes based in the seven deadly sins. We only get to see the aftermath of the murders, which lets the audience's imagination run wild. I love the opening credits sequence with excellent editing and awesome 90's punk music that sets the tone of the film really well. I also like the cool time passage transition with the white font for each day of the week. The cinematography shines during the library scene, where the literary text transitions into scanning the books for clues. The set design for John Doe's apartment lair and all of the crime scenes are effectively creepy and grimy. We also get some incredibly heartbreaking acting from the lust survivor in the interrogation room. This film has multiple great twists that just keep building on one another. Like the first twist that the John Doe killer was actually the gag character photographer from earlier in the movie with Kevin Spacey. Then we get the second twist that John Doe killer cut off the tips of his fingers so he wouldn't leave any fingerprints for the police to trace. Right after John Doe suddenly turns himself with an ultimatum for a full confession and a promise to find the last two bodies (it's questionable to go along with it, but the film executes it well). We get an interesting parallel of John Doe's motivations of caring about the disgusting apathy and crime of the world and doing something about it, just WAY differently than the main 2 detectives. All spoken through an amazing rant by Kevin Spacey during his ride along with the detectives. Finally, the last and most infamous twist is revealed with the iconic "WHAT'S IN THE BOX?!?" Moment that shatters expectations for anyone who wasn't spoiled by this incredibly quotable scene. It's such a gruesome and tragic ending that completes John Doe's grand design and wrath and envy, where John volunteers as envy and baits Brad Pitt's character into wrath. It's a great cautionary tale about the balance between apathy and idealistic obsession. Perfectly summarized by Morgan Freeman's final words of how "'The world is a fine place, and worth fighting for.' I agree with the second part."

5.0 stars; A classic, iconic movie. I saw this movie when it was released 30 years ago. I couldn't remember probably 90% of the movie, but the ending scene is seared onto my brain. I can never unsee that scene. In an era where the usual cop crime film was all about two partners chasing bad guys and developing chemistry between them, this movie had that in spades, but took it to a much higher level by adding shock value. The cast of Brad Pitt, Morgan Freeman, and Gwyneth Paltrow were very young in their careers, but they gave outstanding performances in character building, all of which set you up for that harrowing final scene. This film, along with "Silence of the Lambs", shows up on almost every top 10 mystery/horror films list there is. If you are squeamish at heart, this may not be the film for you. If not, I encourage you to watch this film. If nothing else, just to see how far it advanced this genre of motion picture.

Verdaderamente esto es lo que se busca cuando se trata de un final que deja a todos con la mano en el corazón, la actuación de Pitt en la escena final frente al culpable es perfecta, esa dualidad entre su pecado y la redención que sabe que no puede tener. Amo que deja a merced del espectador cosas importantes que no explica la trama, como ¿era el detective el objetivo desde el principio?, El esfuerzo por mudarse que surgieron del detective, si bien se sospecha que fue por el incidente con el drogadicto, ¿es acaso este incidente fue causado también por el criminal?, ¿qué le quería decir el detective David a Somerset en la escena previa a la búsqueda de los últimos dos pecados?, estas preguntas dejan abrir al espectador el mundo por sí mismo, adaptándolo a su propia interpretación; es perfecta.

The movie Se7en (Seven), directed by the brilliant David Fincher with a first class cast featuring Brad Pitt, Morgan Freeman, Gwyneth Paltrow, and Kevin Spacey, was released today 30 years ago. This suspense thriller keeps you on edge from start to finish, leading to one of the most shocking and unforgettable endings in film history (What's in the box!?). Over time, it has rightfully earned its place as a true cinematic classic. The on-screen partnership between Brad Pitt and Morgan Freeman was remarkable, evolving naturally as their characters unravel a series of dark and disturbingly graphic crimes representing the seven deadly sins. Kevin Spacey delivers a chilling performance as the villain calm, meticulous, and terrifyingly precise. Se7en is more than just a thriller; it’s a masterwork of atmosphere, storytelling, and tension. A must-watch that continues to stand as one of cinema’s greatest achievements.

Se7en is a thriller that has more than earned its place as one of cinema’s most iconic classics. Directed with precision and intensity by David Fincher, the film explores the darkness of human sin through the lens of a chilling serial killer, John Doe, played by Kevin Spacey. His murders are gruesomely methodical, each tied to one of the seven deadly sins. From the gluttony victim—an obese man forced to eat until his stomach ruptures—to the more elaborate punishments that follow, every scene feels deliberately crafted to unsettle. At the heart of the film are two detectives who couldn’t be more different. Morgan Freeman’s Detective Somerset is weary, intelligent, and ready for retirement, while Brad Pitt’s Detective Mills is brash, impulsive, and determined to make his mark. Their dynamic is fascinating; they don’t mesh easily at first, but the investigation forces them into a reluctant partnership. Both performances are phenomenal—Freeman brings a quiet gravitas that anchors the film, while Pitt delivers raw intensity that builds toward one of the most devastating climaxes in movie history. Though Gwyneth Paltrow’s role as Tracy, Mills’ wife, is small, it is absolutely crucial. She embodies warmth and humanity in a film otherwise drenched in darkness, making her eventual involvement in John Doe’s plan all the more devastating. Without her, the emotional gut-punch of the finale would not land nearly as hard. One of the film’s greatest strengths is its pacing. Despite running over two hours, it never drags. The story is expertly structured, with each new murder ratcheting up the tension and pulling the audience deeper into John Doe’s twisted worldview. Fincher uses shadow, rain, and muted tones to create a suffocating atmosphere—one that feels oppressive, grim, and inescapable. The city itself becomes a character: a decaying, nameless urban sprawl where morality feels as compromised as the weather is bleak. The writing deserves immense credit as well. Red herrings, layered mysteries, and the slow revelation of John Doe’s philosophy keep viewers guessing right up until the unforgettable ending. That final sequence, involving the infamous box, is a masterclass in suspense and emotional devastation. Few endings in film history are as shocking, heartbreaking, or perfectly executed. Revisiting Se7en in its 2025 theatrical re-release was a revelation for me. I first saw it years ago on VHS as a kid, and while I remembered the broad strokes, seeing it projected on the big screen renewed my appreciation for its craftsmanship. The atmosphere, the score, and the performances felt larger, heavier, and more impactful than ever. This isn’t just a great thriller—it’s one of the defining films of its genre. Its influence can be seen in countless procedurals and dark dramas that came after, but few have ever matched its chilling precision or emotional power. Verdict: Se7en remains a masterpiece of suspense and dread, flawlessly acted, tightly written, and brilliantly directed. It’s not just recommended viewing—it’s essential.
